name: Walk
description: Baseline precision ground locomotion that turns low-intensity input into stable positioning, interaction alignment, and camera-readable movement without entering faster or committed states.
design_notes: Walk is the control reference for the rest of the locomotion stack, not just a smaller speed value. Define dead-zone policy, analog magnitude handling, digital input fallback, ground_contact_state, step and slope behavior, and the input priority rules for aim, dialogue, interact prompts, and menus. It should be dependable enough for precision positioning, stealth pacing, and close object alignment while leaving run and sprint to handle faster traversal and high-output movement.
design_purpose: Give players precise positioning for exploration, stealth setup, platform alignment, and interaction prompts.
Provide the baseline locomotion state used to compare run, sprint, crouch, aim-walk, and other modifiers.
Make low-speed collision, camera framing, and animation blend thresholds predictable enough for close spaces.
player_fantasy: The player feels fully in control of the character, able to approach, line up, and stop exactly where intended.

parameters.name: walk_pace_units_per_second
parameters.description: Target ground velocity used by the walk locomotion state.

parameters.name: walk_acceleration_curve_id
parameters.description: Curve used when input_vector moves from idle into walking.

parameters.name: walk_deceleration_curve_id
parameters.description: Curve used when the player releases or reverses movement input.

parameters.name: walk_turn_degrees_per_second
parameters.description: Maximum facing rotation while grounded and not locked by animation.

parameters.name: walk_slope_limit_degrees
parameters.description: Steepest walkable ground angle before sliding or blocking movement.

parameters.name: walk_camera_profile_id
parameters.description: Camera follow profile used while the character is in walk state.

parameters.name: walk_dead_zone_threshold
parameters.description: Minimum input magnitude accepted before walk state begins moving the character.

parameters.name: walk_step_offset_units
parameters.description: Maximum precision-step height resolved as walkable ground instead of blocked collision.

edge_cases: Tiny analog stick drift should not move character_id unless it passes walk_dead_zone_threshold and the active control scheme allows analog walking.
Digital keyboard or d-pad input needs a defined walk modifier or it becomes indistinguishable from run.
Slope, stair, and step transitions need a consistent ground_contact_state when the character crosses edges at low speed.
Walk should choose a clear priority when aim lock, dialogue focus, radial menus, or interact prompts also consume input_vector.
Interaction prompts should use the post-collision walk position, not the pre-move target point, when deciding whether the player is aligned.
Networked walking needs client_input_sequence reconciliation without visible foot sliding after correction.
common_bugs: Movement uses render delta instead of fixed-step simulation and becomes inconsistent at unusual frame rates.
Acceleration resets every frame because input_vector is normalized after applying analog magnitude.
Animation blend thresholds do not match walk_pace_units_per_second, causing foot skating.
Ground detection sticks to steep collision and lets characters walk up surfaces beyond walk_slope_limit_degrees.
Dead-zone filtering runs after camera-relative conversion and makes tiny camera changes move the character.
Interact prompts flicker because prompt targeting samples before walk collision and the final resolved position differs.
balancing_notes: Walk pace defines the scale of rooms, interaction spacing, patrol avoidance, and camera comfort.
If walk is too fast, run and sprint lose identity; if too slow, basic traversal feels padded.
High turn rates help accessibility and precision, but can make weighty characters feel frictionless.
accessibility_notes: Support analog input, remappable hold-to-walk, and optional toggle-to-walk for players who cannot hold a modifier.
Avoid tying precise walking to narrow stick angles without calibration and dead-zone settings.

implementation_notes.unity: Store movement_state_id as walk and resolve input_vector, dead-zone filtering, and interaction focus before FixedUpdate motion. Feed target velocity through CharacterController.Move or Rigidbody.MovePosition, apply slope and step checks from contact normals, and drive Animator blend parameters from resolved walk velocity rather than raw input.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep walk as an explicit locomotion_mode_id on CharacterBody2D or CharacterBody3D. In _physics_process, apply dead-zone policy before acceleration, call move_and_slide with floor and step limits, then update AnimationTree blend values from post-collision velocity.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use CharacterMovement walking mode with custom run and sprint modifiers kept outside baseline walk data. For multiplayer, send client_input_sequence and reconcile server corrections without letting animation root motion or interaction focus consume movement twice.
implementation_notes.web: Represent walk state in the fixed simulation loop with input_vector, walk_dead_zone_threshold, ground_contact_state, and surface_material_id. Resolve low-speed collision before render interpolation, then pass final velocity and prompt alignment data into Canvas, Phaser, Three.js, or a custom animation layer.

relationships.reason: Walk provides the baseline ground locomotion state, input filtering, and animation thresholds that run can extend for faster traversal.
A low-cost walk state gives exhausted players a fallback when stamina-gated movement is unavailable.
Smooth follow cameras commonly tune dead zones, damping, and interaction framing around stable walk movement.

example_games.title: The Last of Us
example_games.note: Uses controlled walking for exploration, stealth approach, and close interaction spaces.

example_games.title: Resident Evil 2
example_games.note: Uses deliberate walking pace to support aiming, tension, and room navigation.
